data.title: American Electric Power: This Utility Is Anything But Boring
data.description:
The prospect for lower interest rates, as recently suggested by the Federal Reserve Chairman, could be a boon for capital-intensive utility companies. This could be especially true for those that are well-positioned to benefit from AI-led demand on the grid from power-hungry data centers. While utilities are not typically known for being high-growth enterprises, the new wave of demand could usher in a new era for utilities.

This brings me to American Electric Power (AEP), which I last covered in January, highlighting its operating leverage, strategic growth in regulated utilities and renewable energy, and attractive valuation. AEP has since delivered an 18% total return since my last piece, far surpassing the 8% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. Who says you have to be a tech stock to see high growth?

In this article, I revisit AEP, including recent business results, and discuss why it remains a solid pick for potentially strong long-term returns from here, so let’s dive in!

Why AEP?

American Electric Power is one of the largest regulated utilities in the U.S., serving 5.6 million customers across 11 states. Its operations are diversified across vertically integrated utilities, transmission and distribution, and a growing generation portfolio. AEP is positioned to deliver power to energy-intensive customers at scale with ownership of the largest ultra-high-voltage transmission network in the country.

AEP is hitting on all cylinders as it recently delivered its strongest-ever second quarter operating results over 100 years. This was driven by what management describes as a once-in-a-generation demand growth. Specifically, AEP’s EPS grew by a robust 14% YoY to $1.43. As shown below, AEP’s vertically integrated utilities benefited from rate adjustments and rising industrial and data center load and contributed to most of the EPS gain. At the same time, both retail and wholesale generation contributed another $0.05 to the EPS increase.

Notably, AEP is adding load growth at a pace unmatched in the utility industry. This includes 24 GW of incremental load under firm customer commitments, up from 21 GW previously. These contracts are secured by LOAs (letters of agreement) from hyperscale data centers, crypto mining, and manufacturing reshoring customers in Ohio, Indiana, and Texas.

The agreements lock in financial minimums, much like that of an energy pipeline contract. This protects AEP’s revenue even if customers underutilize their capacity during ramp-up phases. According to McKinsey, data center power consumption is expected to grow at a 9% CAGR between now and the end of this decade.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for 6-8% EPS growth this year. This is supported by the aforementioned contracted load growth as well as 190 GW of additional load requests from customers. To put this into perspective, this is 5x AEP’s current size of 37 GW. While management does not expect for all of this to materialize, even a partial ramp would represent transformational growth for the company. The scale of this opportunity is why management raised its 5-year capital plan from $54 billion to $70 billion.

In the near term, AEP is also positioned to benefit from streamlined filings through a recent Texas bill, which reduces regulatory lag and boosts authorized returns on equity for AEP Texas. Moreover, a recent Oklahoma bill allows deferral of 90% of distribution and general plant spending between rate cases, further reducing regulatory lag.

AEP is supported by a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P and $5.6 billion in liquidity. This is supported by safe FFO/Debt metrics of 14.8% by S&P’s calculation and 13.2% by Moody’s calculation, under their recently revised methodology. AEP also currently yields a respectable 3.3%. The dividend is well protected by a 60.6% payout ratio and comes with a 5-year CAGR of 5.9% and 15 consecutive years of growth.

Admittedly, AEP is no longer a bargain at the current price of $114 with a forward PE of 19.3. As shown below, this sits above its historical PE of 15.8.

Despite the above-average valuation, I believe the premium is deserved. This is considering the robust tailwinds from customer demand as well as favorable legislative outcomes in AEP’s jurisdictions. Over the medium term, I would expect for AEP to be able to deliver 7-9% EPS growth annually for the aforementioned reasons.

This, combined with the 3.3% dividend yield, could result in at least market-level total returns as a base case, with potential upside arising from significant growth in load demands. As shown below, AEP carries a ‘Buy’ rating from Seeking Alpha’s Quant with a score of 4.0 out of 5. This includes A- and B grades for Profitability, Momentum, and Analyst Revisions.

Risks to AEP include regulatory risks as they relate to future base rate outcomes. This could impact the recoverability of AEP’s investments and bottom line profitability. Plus, weather-related factors could disrupt operations and result in unforeseen repair costs. Lastly, AEP has significant exposure to coal-fired facilities. While it seeks to transition to other energy sources, increased political pressure could accelerate that time line and add to decommissioning and environmental remediation costs.

Investor Takeaway

American Electric Power stands out as a high-growth story in the utility sector, driven by surging energy demand from data centers, crypto operations, and industrial reshoring. It has unmatched 24 GW of contracted load growth, supportive regulatory wins, and a $70 billion capital plan position. This positions it for potential sustained high-single-digit EPS growth annually.

While AEP no longer trades at a discount, its premium valuation is justified by long-term visibility into earnings, a well-covered 3.3% dividend, backed by strong credit metrics. As such, AEP remains a compelling pick for investors seeking stable returns with upside potential from AI-driven infrastructure demand.

data.title: Yes, You Can Collect Big Income From Megatrends; Yields +7%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Technology, especially AI, dominates conversations on Wall Street today. Corporations are investing billions in acquisitions, competing fiercely for top talent, and rushing to launch the next breakthrough. AI is no longer just a buzzword; it has become a strategic imperative. As the global workforce adapts and upskills around emerging platforms and tools, technology’s role in business will only deepen. Companies are using more tech today than they did a decade ago, and that trend will accelerate in the next ten years.

But while tech stocks offer strong upside, they also demand good timing to realize usable cash gains. Buy low, sell high – but how high is high enough, and will it go higher? Missing the sell window or a single earnings miss can lead to years of flat or declining prices, and this has happened even for the biggest names.

Supporting the AI revolution is a massive and growing network of data centers. These energy-intensive infrastructures are mushrooming across the globe, and their appetite for power is insatiable. Whether it’s natural gas, nuclear, hydroelectric, or renewables, a consistent and reliable energy supply is required, and large tech companies are locking in multi-year power purchase agreements to secure the energy they need for future growth. This surge in demand is creating long-term tailwinds for utility providers, infrastructure companies, and energy producers

As an income investor, I focus on opportunities that pay reliably – through market ups and downs, regardless of the industry. With AI adoption accelerating and energy demand surging, let’s dive into our top picks positioned to generate regular income from both megatrends.

Pick #1: BST – Yield 7.7%

BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST) is a CEF focused on technology stocks, presenting the Income Method of delivering steady cash flow from the innovations that have shaped, are shaping, and will continue to shape our lives. BST is a strong way to invest in the technology sector while ensuring consistent passive income that you can use as you see fit. The CEF aims to invest ~80% of its assets in the technology sector. Its top holdings may have many similarities with the popular market indices, but notable differences, with BST’s three private-equity positions: Project Debussy (investments in Databricks – Data & AI Platforms), Anduril Industries (autonomous military systems), and Project Picasso (quantum computing).

Over the past ten years, BST has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 in terms of total returns.

With BST, we consistently generate income even during periods of heightened volatility. Here is a look at the income produced from a $10,000 investment in BST since 2015 (without any reinvestment of distributions). Source

About 99% of BST’s distributions in FY 2024 comprised long-term capital gains, making it incredibly tax-efficient for shareholders. The CEF currently pays monthly distributions of $0.25/share, representing a 7.7% annualized yield. We often hear about the high valuations in the tech sector at present. BST sweetens the deal, as it currently trades at a 6% discount to NAV, stretching your dollars further in this futuristic and highly sought-after industry.

Pick #2: EIPI – Yield 7.5%

Extracting hydrocarbons from oil-rich basins is just the beginning. These resources must be transported, processed, and delivered – often across hundreds or thousands of miles – before reaching the end consumer. The same thing applies for electricity generation and consumption. So, the next time you fill your gas tank, turn on the air conditioner, or fire up the grill, remember: that fuel got to you thanks to the critical infrastructure built and maintained by several companies.

Energy infrastructure companies, utilities, and midstream is a terrific business. These companies deploy capital to build infrastructure and monetize it for decades, often through long-term contracts. Rates and fees have attractive CPI-linked escalations, allowing natural protection against inflation.

In recent years, the midstream industry has seen a shift, with labor costs, regulatory overhead, and capital requirements creating a high barrier to entry for emerging competition. It is also noteworthy that established firms are operating with a high degree of fiscal discipline, self-funding their growth CapEx and expanding through tuck-in acquisitions. They are deeply focused on shareholder returns, primarily through share repurchases and distributions.

FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (EIPI) presents a deeply diversified portfolio that maintains 69% of its investments in natural gas, crude oil, and petrochemical production and transmission companies. Source

EIPI holds 93 companies and also focuses on electric power and transmission, as well as other energy-related investments, representing a fund that invests in infrastructure critical to the American economy. With higher energy requirements for data centers that power AI technologies, these companies will continue to enjoy long-term contracts and predictable revenues for the foreseeable future. Approximately 24% of EIPI’s invested assets are in MLPs, enabling investors to benefit from the operations and asset ownership of these strong companies, while avoiding the complexities associated with Schedule K-1.

In May 2024, First Trust Advisors completed the mergers of CEFs – First Trust Energy Income and Growth Fund (FEN), First Trust MLP and Energy Income Fund (FEI), First Trust New Opportunities MLP & Energy Fund (FPL), and First Trust Energy Infrastructure Fund (FIF), to form EIPI. It is noteworthy that while EIPI’s former CEF components were leveraged, EIPI does not use leverage as part of its investment strategy. This ETF has $902 million in assets under management, and actively manages its portfolio, including the pursuit of covered calls to supplement the income from its dividend-paying holdings.

EIPI is writing options on about half its portfolio to earn extra income. The options last ~39 days on average, and are written for prices ~4.22% above, allowing room for modest gains. Altogether, this strategy enhances the income that EIPI generates, allowing a greater cushion for the dividend payments.

We expect EIPI’s dividend consistency to continue, backed by assets that benefit from the higher focus on energy independence and the rapid proliferation of power-hungry data centers.

Conclusion

Some sectors are designed to distribute the majority of their profits as dividends. Others, such as preferred securities and bonds, exist primarily for income generation. And then there are areas like technology, where dividends aren’t the norm. Yet, with the right strategy, even those can become reliable income streams. We don’t shy away from any sector, because every dollar of passive income collected is a win. At High Dividend Opportunities, we cast a wide net and are always ready for buying opportunities.

During the COVID-19 crash, we were buyers. In the 2022 bear market, we were buyers. Through the regional bank crisis and the chaotic “liberation day” selloffs, we remained buyers. Why? Because the steady flow of income from our deeply diversified portfolio means we don’t need to sell in times of fear – we always have cash coming in, ready to be used as we see fit.

That’s the power of income investing. It gives you flexibility, resilience, and peace of mind – a proven recipe for a stress-free retirement.

data.title: The One Call That Could Define My Portfolio For The Next Decade
data.description:
Introduction

Have you ever heard of the "Misery Index?"

It's the sum of the inflation rate and the unemployment rate.

Although it's very simple, it's also very effective because both low inflation with high unemployment and high inflation with low unemployment are undesirable outcomes. The same (obviously) applies to high inflation combined with high unemployment.

Right now, it looks like this:

In 1976, Jimmy Carter used it to criticize President Gerald Ford's economic policies. As we can see above, it hovered close to 13%, an unsustainable number and a sign of poor economic policies. Something that helped Jimmy Carter get elected.

However, once Jimmy Carter made it into office, the Misery Index soared to record highs, pushing the index beyond 20% when inflation was 13.5%.

I'm not bringing this up to turn this article political, but because handling inflation is super tricky. Currently, we are seeing similar developments.

Personally, I believe a big factor that contributed to President Trump's election victory was the high inflation under President Biden. It pushed the Misery Index well beyond 12% and came with substantial supply chain challenges.

Interestingly, President Trump was in office when these supply chain problems started. Now, he is dealing with similar economic issues, including a housing market that is basically locked down due to a multi-decade low in affordability, poor consumer sentiment, and other issues related to elevated rates.

As a result, he blames Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell, whom he has given the nickname "Too Late," as he believes the Fed should have cut rates much more aggressively in the past.

Again, I am not picking sides. All I am saying is that every president has challenges, especially because the economy doesn't reset whenever someone else takes office. It's not like a new game of poker where everyone gets new cards. Presidents continue what the guy before them was working on.

The reason I am bringing all of this up is that President Trump is one of the "victims" of a "higher for longer" inflation environment, which could force him into taking somewhat aggressive measures to broaden economic growth and produce elevated nominal growth.

Right now, this may be my single-biggest thesis for at least two reasons:

  • These policies impact inflation and economic growth, the two biggest drivers of prosperity and stock market returns.
  • If I am right about this, my positioning could allow me to beat the market by a huge margin (I also discussed this in a recent article).

Now, I'm updating my thesis, as I have a lot more data that confirms I'm on the right path.

So, let's get right to it!

Inflation, It's Very Much Still A Problem

In his Truth Social post (the one above), President Trump noted that "There is no Inflation."

That's obviously not true, as all-item inflation is at 2.7%, 70 basis points above the Fed's target and 270% above 0%, which would officially mark "no inflation."

While President Trump is misleading readers, it's not unique. President Biden did the same in 2023 when he noted on Twitter that "Inflation was 0% last month." In this case, the former president meant the month-on-month inflation rate, which can be zero, even if the year-over-year (the one that matters) inflation rate is way above zero.

So, as you can see, I'm not trying to make Trump look bad.

It's just how politics work. I dislike it, but it's how these things go.

The fact of the matter is that inflation is still a problem.

Using findings from Bloomberg's John Authers, who looked into last week's Fed minutes, we see that Fed members are still worried about inflation:

  • Overall inflation remained somewhat above the committee’s two percent longer-run goal.
  • It could take some time for the full effects of higher tariffs to be felt.

Essentially, the Fed believes upside risks are much higher than downside risks:

Officials acknowledged worries over higher inflation and weaker employment, but a majority of the 18 policymakers in attendance “judged the upside risk to inflation as the greater of these two risks,” according to the minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee’s July 29-30 meeting. - Bloomberg

During its latest Jackson Hole meeting, the Fed confirmed my thesis, as it basically said it is prioritizing economic growth over its fight against inflation.

Essentially, they are now aligned with the president:

As I have written in prior articles, this isn't coming as a surprise, as inflation numbers adjusted for outliers (i.e., median CPI and trimmed-mean CPI) have hinted at a bottom close to 3%, more than 100 basis points above the target.

The worst part about this is that energy, which is typically one of the main drivers of inflation, isn't contributing to it. Since 2022, energy has consistently been a deflationary force, as shown below. This makes sense since WTI is now trading in the low-to-mid $60 range, with natural gas (Henry Hub) close to $3.00.

While tariffs are certainly playing a role in short-term inflation, my thesis is mainly built on secular changes, as the favorable inflation period between the Great Financial Crisis and 2021 is unlikely to be repeated. Back then, we had the energy shale revolution producing cheap energy, favorable labor dynamics, globalization (i.e., China producing cheap goods), fewer geopolitical issues, and much more dovish central banks that kept rates subdued. We also had fewer deficit and debt-related problems. But more on that later.

Although AI efficiency gains are certainly deflationary, most of these tailwinds are gone, which brings me to the next chart, which may be one of my favorite charts I have seen this year, as it perfectly visualizes my thesis.

The chart shows 1-year inflation expectations.

  • Between 2009 and 2021, the number averaged less than 2.0%, which means little pressure on consumers and a lot of room for central banks to provide liquidity. It was truly one of the best environments for people to build wealth in a very long time.
  • Before these tailwinds, inflation expectations hovered close to 2% and 3%.
  • Now, we're back in that range, despite all of the "inflation is falling" talk. Hard data is clearly telling a different story.

That's a problem, but not the end of the world.

Inflation Is An Issue, But It Could Be Part Of The Solution As Well

One of the biggest parts of my thesis is that the new administration is fine with neglecting the fight against inflation. By this, I do not mean that they do not care about inflation, but that they know they may have to sacrifice the fight against inflation to broaden economic growth and deal with the national debt/deficit issue, which has become a problem as well.

This is a quote I used in the article I mentioned in the intro:

[...] the Trump administration appears to be embracing that reality with a high-stakes gamble, as it is likely to target (or accept) 4% inflation and high single-digit nominal GDP growth.

While I may sound a bit dramatic, I believe this is a full-blown paradigm shift, one that could—and likely will—renew markets for years.

For investors, this means adapting to a world where cyclical stocks, hard assets, and value will likely outperform growth. - Albert Marko

See, one of the problems is the deficit.

The U.S. is running deficits of more than 5% with an increasingly worrying trend since the Great Financial Crisis. What used to be emergency-like deficits have now become the norm, fueled by programs like the Inflation Reduction Act (Biden) and the just-released One Big Beautiful Bill (Trump).

That's one reason why higher inflation is likely a tool instead of a problem, although I don't expect any politician to say this publicly.

Last week, Lawrence McDonald went on X to show how higher inflation can inflate away the real debt load of a nation:

  • With 3% annual inflation, the real value of $37T debt would halve in about 23-24 years (using ln(2)/ln(1.03) ≈ 23.45 or Rule of 72: 72/3=24) - assumes nominal debt stays constant, ignoring new borrowing or GDP growth."
  • With 2% annual inflation, the real value of $37T debt would halve in about 35-36 years (using ln(2)/ln(1.02) ≈ 35 or Rule of 72: 72/2=36). This assumes nominal debt stays constant, ignoring new borrowing or GDP growth.

This is fine. The key, however, is to kickstart cyclical growth as well. In an environment where inflation is elevated, one needs nominal growth to accelerate as well.

Without accelerating nominal growth, inflation alone can spiral into reduced consumer spending, an imbalance in the wage-price balance, and eroded confidence, which could make debt servicing much harder despite the real debt erosion. It would also be a toxic mix for social tensions, something that worries me a lot, to be very honest.

For example, the bottom 50% of households in the U.S. hold just 2.5% of total wealth, a number that has been stagnating in recent years, as we see below.

Nonetheless, it's a strategy that may be worth the risk.

On top of reducing the real value of the debt, strong nominal GDP growth boosts tax revenues (as incomes and corporate profits rise nominally) without needing tax hikes, which allows governments to cover interest payments and invest in growth through new stimulus if needed.

Essentially, when done correctly, nominal growth acceleration can transform inflation from a debt-relief mechanism into a catalyst for broader economic growth.

As I have written in prior articles, the One Big Beautiful Bill is one way for the Trump administration to boost growth, as it includes permanent tax cuts and expensing for items like CapEx and R&D, investment incentives in properties and manufacturing, no tax on tips and overtime, and an increase in the SALT reduction for households that earn less than $500,000.

So far, it's working!

  • New York and Philadelphia Fed manufacturing surveys (I used the average) show a steep increase in business expectations.

  • The Chicago PMI increased to 47.1 in July. While it remains below the neutral 50 level, the report was very clear about the direction of growth expectations, noting that "This was the largest increase in thirteen months, bringing the index to the highest since March 2025."

  • Small business sentiment is accelerating.

As I said, it's a very risky strategy, but I believe the president is very clear about what he wants. He wants lower rates (despite inflation still being an issue), less regulation, and broader economic growth, which he is trying to achieve through the aforementioned regulation cuts, tariffs, and a potentially weaker dollar.

If he is successful, it could allow him to use higher inflation and higher nominal economic growth to reduce the pressure from the government debt load and the elevated deficit.

How I Am Positioning

This part is not going to include groundbreaking news for most of my regular readers. Nonetheless, I still wanted to add a few lines about my portfolio.

As most will know, I am a long-term investor, which means I do not make short-term bets or make major changes to my portfolio to capitalize on a specific thesis. Essentially, I am not an event-driven investor.

However, I own many stocks that would benefit if I were right about my thesis:

Roughly half of my portfolio is what I would call "cyclical," including my railroad investments in Union Pacific (UNP), Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), my investments in oil producer Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), landowners Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB), as well as housing-related stocks Home Depot (HD) and Carlisle Companies (CSL).

All of these stocks do well over time. However, they tend to really take off whenever cyclical growth rebounds. The last time this happened was in 2021. Since then, I have invested a lot of money in my portfolio, which is why I truly believe that if I'm right about this thesis, I can crush my financial targets in the years ahead.

Moreover, a lot of stocks could benefit, which is why I urge investors to take a closer look at cyclical value and companies with healthy balance sheets, high-margin business models, and/or other ways to beat inflation over time.

If I'm wrong and inflation comes down while economic growth remains sluggish, I will likely underperform the market a bit. However, I do not have the risk of ruining my portfolio, as every company I buy tends to perform well over time, supported by wide business moats, healthy balance sheets, and pricing power.

I do not invest in small cyclical stocks but in proven businesses.

That's one of the reasons why I feel so good about this thesis, as I think the risk/reward for prudent dividend (growth) investors is terrific.

That's also why I expect assets like industrials and (large-cap) value to outperform the market in the years ahead, as broadening economic growth and the "trickle down" effect distribute earnings power more evenly.

That's also why I will spend a lot more time finding suitable investments in the weeks, months, and years ahead, as there's a lot more value to be found on the market beyond the picks I just listed.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

I am convinced we're in a "higher for longer" inflation environment, which is something I have discussed in countless articles since 2021.

This has implications for the economy and the government. Some of which worry me.

The interesting thing is that the Trump administration seems ready to use high inflation to its benefit, as it is prioritizing economic growth over its fight against inflation, which has been hard for many years.

This is a big deal, as this could mean above-average inflation and high single-digit nominal GDP growth, which would be a substantial shift but also the result of developments that started way before Trump or even Biden took office.

While it's risky, it could ease the government's debt burden and boost cyclical stocks, dividend value investment, and hard assets, in general.

Hence, if I'm right, I believe I could generate substantial alpha in the years ahead.

If I'm wrong, my focus on strong, moat-heavy companies keeps me safe, although it could cause me to underperform the market if I fail to make the necessary investments.

We may not like these challenges, but if we play the cards we're dealt in a prudent way, I think we'll be more than fine.

data.title: PACCAR: Strong Mix, Steady Margins, But Upside Capped
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Paccar's (NASDAQ:PCAR) Q2 earnings basically confirmed the company's reputation for best-in-class execution but also explained why the market refuses to push the stock further. Deliveries, Parts, and margins all stood strong this quarter, with execution hitting the upper ends of guidance ranges; however, the forward cadence promises softness: deliveries drop significantly in Q3, tariffs have become a measurable drag on costs, and industry builds are now being revised lower in North America and South America. The end result is a company that, whilst still fundamentally strong, is priced as if visibility on what would catalyze it were better-resourced than it really is.

This, I think, makes for a stalemate. Paccar has the mix, discipline, and structural Parts growth to hold its earnings power through the cycle, but for fresh multiple expansion, investors will want a clearer sign of demand inflection or policy clarity. Otherwise, the premium valuation looks more like a ceiling than a springboard.

About the Company

Paccar operates businesses in its three core businesses: Trucks branded as Kenworth, Peterbilt, and DAF; a Parts distribution network that is now the profitability hub of the company; and a Financial Services business that finances and anchors the earnings base. The balance between a cyclical manufacturing core business and recurring aftermarket streams has given Paccar one of the most stable return profiles of any global capital goods company.

Recent Performance — "Soft topline, resilient margins"

Q2 highlighted both Trucks' cyclical correction in progress and the capacity of Paccar to digest it through Parts and Financial Services. Revenue fell to $7.5bn from $8.8bn a year ago, quite significantly on the back of softer truck deliveries alone: 39.3k units vs 48.4k, an 18.8% YoY fall, but actually beating their guided 38-39k units. North America contributed the most, with reduced freight demand drawing down on orders, and Europe experienced more seasonally associated shutdowns. That equated to Truck segment revenue of $5.24bn, down 20.3% YoY with margins at 8.7% (down 100bps QoQ).

Parts revenue at $1.72bn, up ~3.4% YoY. To me, this growth is a result of a structural trend. Margins were approximately 30% (down 70 bps QoQ), aided in part by distribution and technology investments, and increasing populations of PACR trucks and engines in service. Meanwhile, Financial Services contributed $123mn pretax profit, up ~10.8% YoY and ~1.7% QoQ, driven by stable credit quality and firmer used-truck pricing. Collectively, those segments assisted PCAR to maintain earnings power even as OE deliveries reset lower. The mix resulted in a consolidated TP&O margin of 13.9% (down 413 bps QoQ and 90bps YoY). The offsetting key was Parts, while trucks absorbed two clear headwinds: lower fixed-cost leverage from lower production and some tariff-related headwinds. Management has put surcharges in place to mitigate tariffs, but the timing lag meant Q2 bore the cost. All things considered, Q2 was less about collapse and more about demonstrating that Paccar's diversified mix can smooth the transition into a softer demand environment.

Outlook:

I believe the short term will see some weakness, the medium term will see some pickup, and the long term can see structural gains. Let us examine each segment in isolation:

Trucks "Cyclical trough now, but regulatory tailwinds build the runway."

PCAR has guided Q3 deliveries to about 32k-33k units, another sequential step down. Management indicated that two-thirds of the cut is related to North America and one-third to Europe. The truck segment remains the most cyclical line and that won't change. I expect deliveries to be under some pressure in near term as the freight markets, as well as the sentiment of customers, remain cautious. The weakness we saw in Q2 was most evident in North America, where order volumes have lagged, and in Europe, where seasonal shutdowns further compounded the weakness. Those facts would suggest that we will not see sequential relief in the Q3 data. But I look at this as more of a timing issue than a structural weakness.

Looking ahead, I see three identifiable levers that can reset the topline. The first is regulatory pre-buy activity. The EPA’27 emissions standards will be on the horizon soon enough, and have historically impacted fleet behavior. These episodes typically drive fleets to accelerate purchases 12-18 months before a cutover and pull demand forward in a way that crumples and reshapes the order book. That dynamic has not yet shown up in the formal order data, but based on previous cycles, I expect it will as we move through FY25 and into FY26.

Second, the vocational mix is important. Paccar is less exposed to long-haul freight compared to some peers; instead, a larger portion of its volume comes from construction, municipal, and specialized applications. This provides a natural floor in downturns because government and infrastructure demand tends to be more persistent. The U.S. infrastructure program and related municipal demand should also help it keep this base more stable.

Third, Europe's weakness is not structural. Ongoing seasonal closures in the third quarter will weigh, but Paccar's DAF brand remains competitively positioned, while also taking share in some vocational niches in Europe. We should expect unit volumes to stabilize after production normalizes in Europe.

On profitability, trucks will be the drag in the short term. It's inevitable for lower absorption when deliveries fall, of course, and construction market tariffs flow through faster than surcharges are improving. Management has identified that both will weigh in Q3, and that's a realistic view to me. Beyond that, I expect the slope will change. As the surcharges improve, the gap between cost and price ought to narrow, and if volumes stabilize even moderately, I expect operating leverage to start restoring profitability. The real inflection point, however, will occur when regulatory pre-buy activity increases utilization in FY26. Then trucks can be a margin builder, not a drag.

Parts "The compounding core, less cyclical and more profitable."

The aftermarket business is the stabilizer and compounding growth engine, and I believe the aftermarket will continue to outgrow truck deliveries for the medium term. The rationale is straightforward: the installed base grows with each truck sale, and uptime requisites are growing across fleets. Customers are putting more emphasis on reliability and with PACR's dealer network along with their parts distribution capacity, it can respond very positively to customers' needs compared to many independents.

From a geographic perspective, the Parts demand will typically mirror fleet intensity so this means North America and Europe will remain the anchor markets. With Europe soft on production in Q2, this was offset by the aftermarket pull to some degree, which helped balance. Over time, as PACR develops their Asia Pacific reach, I expect there will be incremental contributions, but North America and Europe will remain core to the aftermarket business. I see low to mid single-digit annual growth as sustainable - even with softer truck god cycles.

Parts margin runs in the low 30s, which is very solid, and well above trucks. While scale economics suggest some incremental benefit from rising volumes. I view this as a segment, given the increasing installed base and distribution reach, ensuring parts continues to grow as a percentage of group operating income. Each percentage point of growth disproportionally increases consolidated margins, which is why I view this segment as a steady driver. In fact, Parts not only will dampen the short-term fluctuations in Truck but, in my view, it will drive PACR's earnings mix towards more attractive quality.

Financial Services "A quiet stabilizer that earns its keep.

Financial Services unit does not make headlines, but I believe its role is strategic. The portfolio builds with truck deliveries and supports sales with tailored financing to fleets. Key, credit quality is strong, and used-truck prices have stabilized, reducing residual value risk. It matters: residual values are a key risk lever in truck finance, and stabilization there removes an earnings headwind. Financing ~233k trucks with $23.3B of assets, the book gives PACR visibility into customer health and buffers earnings volatility.

In the long term, I do not see this as a topline growth driver like Parts is, but as a shock absorber and should grow with truck deliveries. During downturns in markets, access to finance can drive order activity, and during turnarounds, it accelerates with deliveries without adding volatility. On profitability, stable spreads and good credit quality should allow this segment to continue to provide a recurring earnings base that smoothes the group's earnings momentum.

Mix cushions the present, regulation drives the turn.

Overall, I expect the near-term to be muddled: Trucks subdued, Parts compounding, Financial services stable. Although consolidated margins will likely remain under pressure into Q3 which management has already previously warned this is still the trough. From here, as surcharges catch up and absorption recovers its trajectory should improve incrementally.

What's more important to me is the medium-term reset. By FY26, three items should converge: supportive (1) regulatory pre-buy demand for trucks; (2) parts compounding at high incremental margins; and (3) Financial services stable. That combination gives Paccar a shot to come out of this downturn with a better structural mix of earnings and operating leverage that works to its advantage.

Q2 already showed what the portfolio can do with margins in the high-13s even against the truck cycle working against it. I consider that basically a floor. The next two years are about building back towards a higher ceiling supported, to repeat, not just by a truck recovery, but by the growing weight of Parts and the steadiness of Financial services. I expect operating ratios when normalized to gradually rebuild from the Q3 trough towards 14%s over the medium-term.

Peer Group Comparison and Valuation Case

Relative value-wise, Paccar is a solid premium to comps. On P/E, it is ~16.7x trailing, slightly above its five-year average of ~15.2x, and in comparison of Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTRUY) (13.0x) and Volvo (OTCPK:VOLAF) (15.4x). On EV/EBITDA, the difference is wider: PACCAR at ~19.5x vs. Volvo at 10.9x, Daimler Truck at 10.5x, Cummins (CMI) at 10.9x, and Terex (TEX) at 8.0x. Even factoring in Paccar's structurally higher Parts contribution and high margin profile, current multiples are above both history (~12.2x TTM EV/EBITDA) and comps.

The premium multiple can be supported by execution and resiliency, but here again, stability and a reasonable level of future tariff recovery are already being discounted in the market. Without tangible catalysts, be it clarity regarding U.S. trade policy or affirmation of an EPA '27 pre-buy, it is hard to argue that the stock has meaningful rerating potential from here. In other words, Paccar's premium looks secure, but stretched. In my view, PACR is fairly valued at current levels and maintains a Neutral rating.

DCF

DCF also supports a neutral valuation stance. With 8% discount rate, a 24% tax rate, and a 3% perpetual growth rate, derive an intrinsic equity value of $99.25/share, versus the current market price of $102/share, implying a modest (3%) downside. In model assumes steady EBIT growth from $2.7bn in 2025 to $3.4bn by 2029, with free cash flows ranging between $1.7–2.0bn annually. Terminal value estimates based on both perpetuity growth and EV/EBITDA approaches converge at ~$51bn, translating into an enterprise value of ~$44bn and equity value of ~$52bn. The implied IRR of 7% suggests that while Paccar's cash-generation profile is robust but much of its base case fundamental strength is already reflected in the share price.

Risk

The primary, obvious visible risk will be the truck cycle. North American freight fundamentals remain weak, order books are reduced from typical levels, and further significant decline would prolong the revenue and margin trough.

Second, Tariffs are another wild card: the $75 million quarterly headwind is surchargeable, but the lag introduces earnings volatility, and any unexpected increase in raw material prices could erode pricing power.

Third, regulatory and competitive dynamics in the truck industry remain fluid. While EPA’27 pre-buy is expected to lift volumes, any shift in implementation timing or technology costs could alter the demand pattern.

Last but not least, any unanticipated change and modification in broader industrial policy risk: trade barriers, subsidy regimes, or shifts in U.S./EU emissions rules could impact both demand timing and margin realization.

Conclusion

Paccar continues to deliver at a level that few others within the industry can keep up with. Q2 was strong, Parts is reporting record revenue, and cost control remains intact. The reality, however, is that deliveries will decline, tariffs are taking a bite out of costs, and industry builds are being reduced. With the stock already trading premium to history and far ahead of global comparables, valuation is capturing much of that excellence. Until we get firmer guidance on tariffs or the EPA'27 pre-buy, it is hard to make the case for fresh upside. I thus have a Neutral rating on PACR.

data.title: Markets Consolidate After The Big Moves Before The Weekend
data.description:
Overview

The US markets responded dramatically to Federal Reserve Chair Powell's speech at Jackson Hole before the weekend. In today's late August session devoid of much news, the markets are consolidating. The dollar is in narrow ranges, and there may be some more corrective upticks in North America today. Emerging market currencies are more mixed. The PBOC set the dollar's fix lower by a relatively large amount (~0.23%) to a new low for the year, and the index of Chinese property developers rose as much as 3% earlier today amid expectations of new measures to support the real estate sector.

The Fed funds futures market is discounting now not only the high probability of a Fed cut next month but also another one before the end of the year and at least three cuts next year. Moreover, the early forecasts for the next US jobs report (September 5) project another weak report and another rise in the unemployment rate. With London closed, the cash Treasury market awaits the US open, but the futures market points to slightly firmer yields. Most European benchmark 10-year yields are 2-3 bp higher. All the large equity markets in the Asia Pacific region report, led by 2% gains in China's CSI 300 and Taiwan's Taiex. South Korea's Kospi and Hong Kong's Hang Seng rallied more than 1%. Europe's Stoxx 600 and US index futures are nursing small losses. Gold rallied almost 1% before the weekend, to almost $3379, but is softer near $3367 in Europe. October WTI has crept up to extend its advance for the fourth consecutive session. It is near $64 after putting in a low last week, near $61.50.

USD: The Dollar Index posted a bearish outside down day ahead of the weekend, in reaction to Fed Chair Powell's comments. It made a new low for the month slightly below 97.60 and settled below the up trendline drawn off the July and earlier August lows. It is trading quietly, consolidating in a narrow range (less than 30 ticks below 98.00). The US economic calendar is light this week, and when everything is said and done, the derivatives market is still pricing in a high probability (~85%) of a rate cut next month and has two quarter-point cuts priced in for this year. Rather than the high frequency economic data, the market may be more sensitive to the Fed officials speaking, including Dallas Fed President Logan, who speaks today, and some have suggested she may be under consideration to succeed Powell as chair. NY Fed's Williams also speaks today. His comments often reflect the leadership of the Fed. Governor Waller, who dissented in favor of a cut last month, speaks on Thursday. He is also thought to be under consideration for the chair, but his unambiguous endorsement of the central bank's independence may jeopardize his chances, especially if Governor Cook's seat becomes vacant. The coming weekend is extended due to the Labor Day holiday, and then the August jobs report comes into focus. The early estimates suggest another soft nonfarm payroll rising by about 85k. The unemployment rate is seen rising to 4.3% from 4.2%.

Euro: This week's highlights include M3 and the lending figures, confidence surveys, and the ECB's inflation expectations survey. The euro is not particularly sensitive to these reports. The euro posted large outside up day ahead of the weekend. It made a new high for the month, near $1.1745. The high from late July was near $1.1795, but the multi-year high set on July 1 was closer to $1.1830. Not only did US rates tumble before the weekend, but the US two-year premium over Germany settled below 175 bp, its lowest since early April. The euro is trading in a narrow range in the upper end of last Friday's range (~$1.1690-$1.1735). The lack of follow-through buying could see some more late longs get squeezed out. Support may be in the $1.1660-80 area. Before the weekend, Germany's Q2 GDP was revised down to -0.3% quarter-over-quarter from -0.1%. The August IFO survey showed a new high in expectations component since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, but the current assessment (86.4 vs. 86.5) has been practically flat for the last several months.

CNY: The PBOC is threading the needle. It has steadily lowered the dollar's reference rate, introduced more flexibility into setting of said reference rate, and the yuan remains broadly stable against the greenback. With a couple minor exceptions, the dollar has been in a CNY7.15-CNY7.20 trading range for nearly three months. Today was another exception. The greenback briefly slipped below CNY7.1490, a new low for the year, encouraged by a sharply lower dollar fix. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at CNY7.1161 (CNY7.1321 before the weekend). The setting of the daily reference rate is one of the chief ways that Beijing manages the exchange rate. Yet, the Chinese hawks want to talk about intervention as if all of the state-owned bank activity is "stealth" activity on behalf of the central bank, as if trade flows (estimates suggest a large portion is still conducted in dollars) and capital flows do not require currency conversion. After surging to CNH7.2240 before the poor US employment report on August 1, the greenback settled into a CNH7.1680-CNH7.1980 range in recent weeks. It was frayed before the weekend, as the greenback set a low near CNH7.1665. After today's fix, it fell to nearly CNH7.1490.

JPY: The hawkish anticipation for Powell and the rise in US yields helped lift the dollar to almost JPY148.80, its highest level since August 1, before Powell spoke. The greenback reversed course and fell to a new low for the week, slightly below JPY146.60. It posted its lowest settlement since July 23. Initial support is around JPY146. In today's consolidation, the dollar has been confined to about JPY146.80-JPY147.55. The corrective pressures do not appear exhausted, but the JPY147.70-JPY148 may be sufficient. The highlight this week comes on Friday: August Tokyo CPI (expected to have moderated) and a drop in July industrial production, while Japanese consumers continued to shop, with a smaller gain in retail sales after the 0.9% jump in June.

GBP: Sterling looked weak. It had slipped below $1.34, for the first time in two weeks. Then Powell spoke and sterling rallied to almost $1.3545. It posted its biggest gain in three months. The high from late July and mid-August, around $1.3600. Sterling has not traded above $1.3535 today and may be headed to the $1.3450-60 area as some momentum traders move to the sidelines. It is a quiet week for UK data, mostly housing data, consumer credit and supply.

CAD: The US dollar rose to CAD1.3925, a three-month high before Powell spoke. It was sold to CAD1.3815 before the weekend. It is in almost a 25-tick range above CAD1.3820 so far today. Nearby support is around CAD1.38, where the (61.8%) of the rally from the month's low (Aug 7 ~CAD1.3720) and the 20-day moving average converge. Still, corrective pressures could see a move toward CAD1.3850-60. Canada is among the last of the G10 countries to report Q2 GDP. The first estimate is due at the end of the week. Bloomberg has two surveys. The first (ECFC) shows a median forecast in its survey of a 0.5% contraction. The second (WECO) shows a median forecast of a 0.7% contraction. The monthly GDP contracted in both April and May by 0.1%. According to the median forecast (WECO) the economy may have grown by 0.1% in June.

AUD: The broad setback for the greenback arrested a four-day slide by the Australian dollar ahead of the weekend. The Aussie had fallen to $0.6415, the low for the month. It proceeded to recover and briefly traded slightly above $0.6500, a four-day high, and settled above the 20-day moving average (slightly above $0.6485). It is one of the best performing G10 currencies today and made a marginal new high to about $0.6505, while holding above $0.6470. Initial resistance is seen in the $0.6510-20 area. Tomorrow's minutes from the Reserve Bank of Australia's meeting earlier this month, when it delivered a quarter-point cut will be looked at for clues about the timing of the next move.

MXN: The dollar had been coiling in narrow ranges against the peso most of last week. Then with Powell's comments at Jackson Hole, the dollar broke lower. It fell to a new low for the week near MXN18.57. It held earlier today, and the greenback has approached MXN18.65. It may not have completed the squeeze higher, but we suspect it has come close and anticipate resistance closer to MXN18.70. Ahead of the weekend, Mexico reported lower than expected inflation figures for the first half of August. The headline rate fell (0.02%) for the first time this year, while the year-over-year pace edged up to 3.49% from 3.48%. The core rate rose by 0.0%, a smidgeon less than expected and the year-over-year rate was shaved to 4.21% from 4.22%. Mexico reports the Q2 current account today. Since the pandemic, Mexico runs a trade deficit (though this year it has swung into surplus). Its current account deficit averaged less than 0.3% of GDP in the past two years and is projected to be about that size this year. The average current account deficit in the 2017-2019 period was almost 1.4% of GDP. As a consequence of US immigration policies and environment, worker remittances (average $5.4 bln a month last year) have already begun to trend lower.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TotalEnergies: A Company In Transition Towards A Green Agenda
data.description:
TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) is a French company fully dedicated to the oil and gas industry. Its main business lines include oil exploration and extraction, primarily offshore, liquefied gas, and LNG. This latter segment encompasses the entire processing and value chain of this fuel, which also includes the midstream and upstream segments. Finally, in addition to the general hydrocarbon business, there is a growing segment dedicated to the production of renewable energy. While this report seeks to provide a comprehensive analysis of the company, I want to draw attention to two points that I consider key in a process that seeks to transition to more sustainable energy.

In this regard, the first change I'd like to mention is the company's quest to increase the share of LNG relative to oil extraction. Second, the longer-term transition to energy generation based on a more sustainable matrix and with a much greater share of green energy.

Regarding the transformation the company wants to undertake, I would like to emphasize, from my perspective, that the changes regarding LNG make sense for the company and adapt it to a reality where environmental regulations are becoming increasingly restrictive, particularly in the case of the European Union. However, regarding renewable energy, the situation is much more complex as it entails a radical change in the company's business model. Transitioning from being an oil & gas company to renewable energy requires a different know-how and expertise than Total has amply demonstrated in recent decades in the fossil fuel segment.

Thesis

TotalEnergies is one of the leading European companies in the oil and gas industry. It currently maintains operations around the world, but primarily with a presence in Europe, Africa, and the now largely neglected South and North America. As can be seen below, just over 70% of its revenue comes from Europe.

It's interesting to note that over the last decade, the company has shifted from a strong focus on oil production to a mixed business, with LNG representing 44% of the company's total revenue in 2024 and oil accounting for 43%. The remaining 13% is accounted for by low-carbon energy production.

From my perspective, the company's guidance regarding the increasing relative weight of LNG production to the detriment of oil is extremely relevant. TotalEnergies expects that by 2030, 50% of its total revenue will come from LNG production and 20% from renewable energy. This, in my view, responds to the European Union's initiatives to ensure a major shift in the continent's energy mix in the coming decades. According to the European Green Deal, launched in 2019, the member countries of the European Economic Community committed to achieving a 50% emissions reduction by 2030. This has entailed a series of measures, regulations, and subsidies by member countries to achieve this goal, which, in my view, is somewhat ambitious.

This quest by Total to increase the weight of LNG relative to oil is closely related to the perspective that natural gas can serve as a bridge in the transition toward a complete restructuring of the global energy matrix. In this regard, I would like to emphasize that the vision of the major developed countries for the world to shift to 100% sustainable energy in the short term has proven to be extremely ambitious. In addition, there is a technical and productive factor, where the change implies longer than expected times. In this scenario, natural gas, which generates far fewer emissions than oil, serves as a great bridge to the goal of a world powered entirely by renewable and sustainable energy.

One of the analyses I find extremely important is the relationship between GDP growth over recent years and the increase in electricity demand. Now, it's worth asking how this increase in global electricity demand is being met. As can be seen, LNG growth has been significantly higher than that of oil. While renewable energies represented a greater increase, it's important to note that the initial level value from which each one starts is much lower compared to LNG. If this trend continues, Total's management's vision for the coming decades is interesting.

I now want to turn to the company's most ambitious plan, which involves an almost complete restructuring over the coming decades. Total expects that in the long term, LNG and oil production combined will represent just 27% of revenue. They estimate the remainder will be distributed between Low-Carbon Molecules and electricity generation, which could represent 50%.

Beyond the fact that the plan not only strikes me as ambitious, while radically changing the company's business model from a conventional oil company to a renewable energy generator, what raises red flags for me is the amount of CAPEX the company plans to allocate to this change of direction.

The project involves a gradual modification of the company, the impact on cash flow can already be seen. While it is true that Total estimates its CAPEX to be $17.8 billion this year, with 34% of that amount allocated to the start-up of new oil and LNG projects, there is an amount that cannot be overlooked. From the company's perspective, approximately 27% of this CAPEX will be allocated to low-carbon energy generation. This percentage represents a total of $4.8 billion, a significant amount that is not distributed to shareholders.

Another point I'd like to highlight is that, relative to the industry, Total's margins are generally lower. It's true that compared to other companies in the S&P 500 or the Euro Stocks 600, the company's margins are very good. However, when measured against industry values, as shown in the SeekingAlpha Profitability segment below, the performance is not as spectacular. In fact, I've previously covered smaller companies that have certainly outperformed.

2Q2025 Results

In this segment, I would like to briefly summarize the company's results reported in Q2 2025 and compare them with those seen in prior periods. Before beginning, I would like to point out that the key financial revenue indicators deteriorated quarter-over-quarter and compared to the same period last year, primarily due to the drop in the international price of fossil fuels, primarily oil.

Regarding the company's reported results, revenues in the second quarter of 2025 were $44.7 billion, a 7% drop compared to the $47.9 billion reported in the previous quarter. If we analyze the trend for the first half of 2025 and compare it with the same period in 2024, we see an 8% drop, virtually the same as on a quarter-by-quarter basis.

A similar trend was seen in Total's EBITDA, which reached $9.7 billion. In the first half of the year, the company's EBITDA reached $20.2 billion. This not only represented an 11% drop compared to the same period in 2024, but when compared to the current EV value, it can be calculated that Total's EV/EBITDA ratio stands at 4.38x. Admittedly, this sounds like a low value, but the recent decline in results, coupled with the plan to modify and invest in alternative energy, could raise orange flags.

I want to now describe that cash from operations (CFFO) showed a value of $6.6 billion in 2Q2025. Compared to the first half of the year, the amount was $13.6 billion, which again represents a decrease compared to the same period last year. If we compare this latest annualized value with the company's market cap, we see that the P/CFFO ratio is around 5.06x, which is an attractive value, but there are companies with much more attractive multiples that are undergoing extremely less ambitious change processes.

Below I share the results of the 2Q2025 earnings release published by the company that stood out the most in financial and economic terms.

Valuation

I now want to move on to a valuation using multiples, using the main European companies in the oil & gas sector as my primary comparables. These peers are found in SeekingAlpha's Peers segment, with the exception of Italy's Eni. For these European companies, I decided to maintain the selection made by Petrobras SA, based on their similar market capitalization, geographic diversification, and similar vertical integration. I deliberately excluded the main American companies, as they show a larger size, a different tax structure, and a greater concentration in North American basins.

The ratios shown in the following table were extracted from SeekingAlpha's own Peer segment. For the analysis, I decided to use PE, EV/EBITDA, P/FCF, and P/BV as valuation ratios. For each ratio, I calculated a target value based on a market capitalization-weighted average, and with that target value, I calculated a target price for each ratio.

Once these values ​​were obtained, and based on a weighted average (the weighting factors of which can be seen in the table itself), I arrived at a valuation of the company. I decided to prioritize EV/EBITDA as a relevant multiple over P/BV because in companies of this size and with so many business lines, the book value can be distorted, affecting this result. From the analysis performed, it can be seen that, compared to its main European peers, Total appears to be trading almost at Fair Value. If we add to this the ambitious reform plan, the HOLD rating I gave the company is somewhat justified.

Risks

Finally, I would like to briefly summarize the potential risks the company could face. Undoubtedly, those tied to the current downward trend in oil prices could generate problems in the short and medium term. The possibility that the new US presidential administration will raise import tariffs, especially considering that China is one of the main victims of these measures, could lead not only to a drop in crude oil demand. What would be far worse would be if the conflict escalated into a trade war between the major economic powers, resulting in a drop in GDP. As we saw at the beginning of the report, energy generation and the increase in demand for LNG and green energy are closely linked to global economic growth.

However, I don't want to go into this point in depth, which I've already discussed with other oil and gas industry companies I've covered, such as Permian Resources and APA Resources, and even in my recent report on YPF and Ecopetrol. What I'd like to highlight is a trend that could mark a path Total could take. Recently, EU countries have allocated increasingly large amounts of money to the development of renewable energy and the growth of these industries. These amounts of money have led to the creation of new companies, but have also been partially allocated to many of the continent's oil companies.

This has led to the initiative for a future of sustainable energy generation by companies that have long been explorers and producers of fossil fuels emerging as a search for this money. The fact that European companies in the oil and gas sector tend to become chasers for EU subsidies poses a potential risk, for me. On the one hand, it's true that revenues increase in the short term because investment projects can be carried out at relatively low costs. But on the other, it creates a dependence on a political incentive that raises doubts about profitability once it is no longer in place. The day countries remove their subsidies, the possibility of projects generating sustainable revenues raises a question mark in my mind.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Still A Hold, But Monthly Dividend And Improvements Are Attractive For Income Investors
data.description:
Introduction

As an income investor, BDCs play an integral part in my investment strategy. Previously shunned by investors as BDCs are considered riskier investments, the last few years the sector (BIZD) seems to have grown on them.

This is evident by the amount of analysts on Seeking Alpha that now tout them. When I started here in 2023, it seemed like I was the only one that found them to be not just for short-term income, but for long-term as well.

One reason is likely due to higher for longer interest rates, which have resulted in some strong performances amongst standouts in the sector. However, with increased macro uncertainty, I've become quite cautious of BDCs in recent months.

But Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC), despite uncertainty, continues to impress with each quarter. In this article, I discuss CSWC's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors looking to own BDCs for income may consider Capital Southwest.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Capital Southwest back in May in an article titled: Latest Quarter Confirms Why This Is A Top Income BDC.

During their Q4 earnings, CSWC saw their bottom line decline quarter-over-quarter and the previous year to $0.56. Partly, this was due to lower overall base rates and a $0.05 related to the exit of their CEO.

Despite this, the BDCs' performance was solid, as they managed to see double-digit growth in their top line from $178.1 million to $204.4 million.

Although the drop in Nll resulted in dividend coverage falling below 100%, I remained confident in the company's ability to sustain their dividend due to their undistributed taxable income.

Since, CSWC has had a good performance relative to the S&P (SP500), who's up over 10% compared to 8.52% for the BDC.

Impressive Q1 Performance

In recent months, I've grown weary of some BDCs' ability to sustain their dividends. And dividend cuts over the past year, projected lower base rates, and recent reports show the potential for further economic weakening.

Despite me considering most BDCs holds due to macro uncertainty and suspected lower base rates, Capital Southwest has continued to impress me amidst uncertainty.

The most recent quarter saw their bottom line climb $0.05 from the previous quarter to $0.61.

Although pre-tax net investment income is down year-over-year on a per share basis from $0.64, in totality this actually increased slightly from $31.1 million to $31.2 million.

While some investors get caught up on the per share basis, I like to see how a BDC's bottom line grew on a total basis, as this tells more of their credit quality.

For comparison, Ares Capital (ARCC) saw their bottom line decline on a per share and total basis. This retracted from $0.62 to $0.49. And in totality, net investment income fell from $386 million to $342 million.

While I view ARCC & CSWC as two of the highest quality BDCs in the sector, CSWC's performance in recent months has moved them in 1st place of my favorite BDCs, long held by Ares Capital.

And this has to do with how management has impressively recognized gains harvested from their equity portfolio, which continues to be a secret weapon.

During Q1, CSWC saw their undistributed taxable income increase from $0.79 to $1 per share due to harvested gains of $27.2 million. Higher interest and dividend income also played a role in their top and bottom line growth.

Over the last two quarters, gains from their equity portfolio have amounted to $41.3 million. This is how the BDC was able to offset lower investments during the quarter, which amounted to $115 million, down from $146.2 million the prior quarter.

The $115 million was invested across 3 new companies and 12 existing ones, bringing their total to 122 companies. Furthermore, leverage, payment-in-kind income, and non-accruals also improved.

Improved Balance Sheet

Capital Southwest already had one of the lowest leverage ratios amongst its peers, well below the peer average of 1.19x. Leverage improved to just 0.82x, down from 0.89x in Q4.

In comparison to some of my favorite BDCs in terms of quality and performance, only Fidus Investment (FDUS) had a lower ratio of 0.80x.

ARCC's stood at 1.02x. Moreover, Capital Southwest has no maturing debt until October of next year, with only $150 million due. And liquidity was plentiful, with $444 million in cash and cash equivalents.

I touched on this in my previous article, but the BDC received final approval for their second SBIC license, which gives them additional access to capital up to $175 million over time.

So, CSWC seems to be in a favorable position to navigate potential headwinds from a weakening economy or to take advantage of growth opportunities that present themselves.

Monthly Dividend Switch

In June, Capital Southwest decided to switch to paying a monthly dividend, making the stock even more attractive for the long term. The company declared a $0.1934 dividend. And with the switch, I wonder if peers will follow in their footsteps.

While I remain cautious on the sector due to economic weakening, CSWC remains in a comfortable position to maintain its dividend as a result of their robust liquidity in the form of spillover.

As previously mentioned, this increased from $0.79 to $1 per share in the latest quarter, giving them some flexibility should the FED lower interest rates in the coming months.

NAV & Non-Accruals

NAV in the quarter saw an $0.11 drop from $16.70 as a result of restricted stock issuance. On an annual basis, the NAV decline was modest, with a $0.01 decline to $16.59.

Another reason CSWC's latest quarter was impressive is the BDC saw sequential improvements in their non-performing loans on a fair value basis.

This is something I had been critical of in previous articles and suggested, with the economy seemingly weakening, this was something investors should keep an eye out for in the coming quarters.

But these improved to 0.8%, down from 1.7% in the previous quarter. In Q2, these were as high as 3.5%. And the decline is a further testament to CSWC's credit quality, something that has remained resilient during the high interest rate environment.

Premium Valuation

As previously mentioned, the potential for additional economic weakening and lower base rates has kept me cautious on many BDCs.

However, with the way CSWC has performed, seeing improvements in their non-accruals, if I had to suggest one stock to stay invested in that I thought could navigate and avoid a potential dividend cut, Capital Southwest would be it.

However, their premium with a P/NAV ratio of 1.36x continues to keep me at a hold. Moreover, if we do see interest rate cuts this year, the sector could get cheaper as portfolio yields decline as a result.

This would likely cause further dividend coverage tightening, which could result in a potential pull back.

Furthermore, CSWC's current P/NAV ratio is currently one of the highest in the sector in comparison to some of the higher-quality peers:

Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.62x

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL): 1.10x

Ares Capital: 1.12x

So, although CSWC is attractive from an income standpoint, I suggest investors wait for a potential pullback before adding, preferably around $20 per share.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite their improvements, economic uncertainty remains high. Moreover, it's likely we could see tariffs start to show up in reports in the coming months.

And although this would likely result in the FED holding rates for longer, this could impact borrowers, causing a spike in non-performing loans as a result.

Moreover, this could also cause PIK income to increase as borrowers could see financial distress from higher for longer interest rates.

While I expect Capital Southwest to successfully navigate due to their financial position, this would likely limit upside and cause their share price to decline.

But if you're an investor currently living off income and are adamant on holding or investing in BDCs, Capital Southwest would be my suggestion.

data.title: Red Violet: Undercovered Stock But Potentially Priced
data.description:
Introduction

In my Nutanix article, I mentioned that I look for technology stocks with the following characteristics: recurring revenue, high gross margins, profitability, and expectation beats. Red Violet (NASDAQ:RDVT) is another company I find excellent on all those fundamental measures. But what's even more surprising is that the stock is simply not given much attention by Wall Street. According to FactSet, there was one analyst actively covering the stock in July and only two this month (August). This means potential future growth may be overlooked by the market, making it a great stock in the long term, given its strong fundamentals and growth prospects. As I detail later, near-term developments seem to be priced in.

Business

Industry

Red Violet primarily operates in the data analytics industry with a focus on fraud detection and prevention. Their technology focuses on providing real-time identification of people (as well as businesses and assets), so I would like to narrow my focus on the fraud detection market as the data analytics industry is quite broad.

As we can see from the chart above, the overall industry is yet to reach its exponential growth stage. As digital transactions continue to become more mainstream, costs from surging cybersecurity fraud continue to rise. This, in turn, increases the demand for preventive solutions, with the North American market projected to grow nearly 200% by 2030. Given that nearly all of Red Violet's revenue is generated in the United States, the industry backdrop is supportive of high-growth potential.

Performance

Red Violet's infrastructure, CORE, is a cloud-native AI/ML-driven platform that supports their two primary customer-facing solutions: IDI and FOREWARN. IDI is an analytics provider that helps organizations in various sectors (finance, healthcare, defense, etc.) to make data-driven decisions. The other platform, FOREWARN, which is also the more popular of the two, is an app-based solution for the real estate sector, providing background information on potential customers. As of last quarter, FOREWARN made up 97.3% of total billable customers.

So how have these services performed? Quite well. In the last six quarters, revenue and EBITDA have beaten expectations, driven by customer growth (as seen above). With a 96% revenue retention rate as of last quarter, Red Violet continues to generate recurring revenue from a sticky customer base, as pricing contracts have represented 74–78% of total revenue since Q1 2024. As time goes on, I would like to see revenue retention climb above 100% and pricing contracts represent around 80% of revenue as further proof of a strong business model. But for now, I think the revenue figures are signs of a favorable growth outlook.

Additionally, Red Violet's strong margins are a sign of steady growth. Often, adding new customers drives up costs and keeps margins stagnant, but this has not been the case so far. Gross and EBITDA margins have been improving consistently YoY. As of Q2, Red Violet reported an all-time high of 84% adjusted gross margin. Healthy margins are a sign of sustainable growth, favorable for long-term prospects.

It's also important to note that Red Violet retains a healthy balance sheet, with $38.8 million worth of cash equivalents and only $2.9 million worth of debt.

For the foreseeable future, FOREWARN continues to be the main focus for management. In their recent Q2 earnings call, management noted they wanted to invest heavily in expanding FOREWARN to new industries beginning in 2026, a logical move in my view given the strength of the platform.

Catalysts

Government Expansion

Management wants to expand into emerging markets where Red Violet currently does not have a strong penetration. This primarily includes the government vertical, an untapped source of large non-cyclical revenue. Back in March 2024, Red Violet appointed Jonathan McDonald (previously at TransUnion) to lead the public sector division and execute on this initiative. Government contracts in identity intelligence usually carry longer integration cycles and higher switching costs, enhancing customer stickiness. Management signaled this expansion to be a key driver in 2026, and I expect government contracts to help drive revenue expectation beats in future quarters.

Partnerships

Given that Red Violet is a smaller name in the IT industry, partnerships make it easier to cross-sell products and reach a larger addressable market. Back in May, Red Violet announced an integration of its IDI solution with Ping Identity to deliver no-code identity orchestration for enterprises' digital workflows, making it easier to use Red Violet's platform. Although IDI is the smaller and less popular segment of Red Violet's brand, it still holds product value since it is backed by the CORE platform. A strategic partnership allows for growth without outsized spending on marketing a less popular product, supporting additional top-line revenue growth without compressing margins. As Red Violet continues to expand into verticals and acquire more customers, I would expect to see more partnerships that would lift revenue through cross-selling in future quarters.

Valuation

The street price target, according to FactSet, is currently $56. But from a relative standpoint, Red Violet has considerably higher multiples than its peers.

Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

Given the company's high multiples, I look at the intrinsic valuation for Red Violet through a discounted cash flow analysis.

From the years 2025-2027, in my analysis, I used CapIQ street estimates for revenue growth, margins, CapEx, and D&A. I project changes in NWC as a % of sales. Given there was no data available for 2028-2029, I used my own projections to reflect my views on the company's growth prospects. I assumed a 16% revenue growth rate in 2028, 2029, a bit higher than former years as I anticipate Red Violet growing in size as it captures a larger market share. For EBITDA margin, I assume it stays steady and continues to follow a gradual but increasing trend of better margins. And for EBIT margin, I assume it grows to 17% by the end of 2029 to reflect the benefits of scale from a larger business in the future. Using these assumptions, I get an implied share price of $39.36, not far, but still below where the stock trades at.

It's important to note that technology companies often trade past their fundamental value. But in this case, I see this as a sign that near-term developments are priced in. Most of Red Violet's near-term growth prospects come from the fact that it is simply growing its revenue and nothing overly complicated (no new products, etc.). However, I do see fundamentals accelerating to where the price is justified or the price comes down near the $39 range, which would make it a good buy. However, since it trades at a high premium to peers as well, I see Red Violet as a HOLD for now.

Risks

Data Security

Given that Red Violet's products have access to enterprises' confidential data, any data breach would harm the brand image of Red Violet, making it hard to gain new customers and hurting existing client relationships. Violations of data security may also go against federal regulation, which could lead to heavy fines and impact overall profitability.

Market Concentration

Most of Red Violet's revenue is concentrated in the US. This means the company is exposed to macroeconomic and regulatory conditions that adversely affect the US, causing Red Violet to fall behind competitors who are diversified geographically.

Conclusion

Overall, Red Violet remains a great business. I would like to see the company develop a larger moat by securing more contracts, particularly with the government, in 2026. However, its current price indicates that it is not exactly the right time to buy, but future price drops (assuming sound business quality) or accelerating fundamentals would push my thesis to a buy.

data.title: Boeing Vs. Airbus: Order Strength Meets Delivery Weakness
data.description:
Boeing (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY) are facing an uphill battle to satisfy demand for airplanes. Both jet makers saw a significant increase in their stock price. At the same time, we do note that Boeing is facing a cap imposed by the FAA on production of its best-selling 737 model, and Airbus is plagued by aerospace supply chain issues. As the investment case for both jet makers is predicated on the ability to continue increasing production, it is of paramount importance to keep careful track of how orders as well as deliveries are tracking.
